stories.headline: Trump Administration Threatens Jeffco Schools With Loss of $90 Million Over Transgender Policies
stories.summary:
The Trump administration sent Jefferson County Public Schools letters of "impending enforcement action" Friday, escalating a federal effort to revoke tens of millions of dollars in funding over the district's policies on transgender students. The U.S. Department of Education's Office of Civil Rights has accused Jeffco of violating Title IX by allowing transgender students to compete in female sports and use girls' bathrooms, allegations the district denies.

District leaders are now appealing to Gov. Jared Polis, Attorney General Phil Weiser and state education Commissioner Susana Córdova to publicly back Jeffco and participate in any legal defense. In a letter, departing Superintendent Tracy Dorland and incoming interim Superintendent Rob Stein warned the district cannot mount a legal fight alone, particularly after absorbing $40 million in budget cuts that caused layoffs and program eliminations.

Polis's office said it is evaluating next steps, while a Weiser spokesperson declined to comment and the Colorado Department of Education did not respond to a request for comment. Federal officials, for their part, said Jeffco "refused to correct its widespread and blatant Title IX violations" and ended negotiations, according to an Education Department news release.

stories.headline: Avalanche Sign Defensemen Brent Burns, Brett Kulak to New Contracts
stories.summary:
The Colorado Avalanche locked up two veteran defensemen Friday, signing Brett Kulak to a five-year deal with a $4.5 million cap hit and Brent Burns to a one-year contract with an $850,000 base salary and potential bonuses that could bring the total to roughly $3 million. The signings ensure that all six of Colorado's top defensemen from last season are now under contract for 2026-27.

Kulak, 32, joined the Avalanche at the trade deadline from Pittsburgh and was considered a steady, defense-first presence capable of playing on the second or third pairing. Burns, 41, had 12 goals and 35 points in 82 games last season and sits at 1,007 consecutive games played, second all-time behind Phil Kessel — a record he could break in Game 58 of the upcoming season.

The moves follow a flurry of offseason trades in which Colorado dealt Valeri Nichushkin, Ross Colton and the rights to Jack Drury to clear cap space. The Avalanche now carry 11 forwards, six defensemen and two goalies on one-way contracts, with approximately $5.63 million in cap space remaining as the NHL Draft opened Friday in Buffalo.

stories.headline: Denver Summit FC Unveils $25 Million CommonSpirit Performance Center in Centennial
stories.summary:
Denver Summit FC officially opened the CommonSpirit Performance Center in Centennial on Friday, a roughly 20,000-square-foot facility budgeted at approximately $25 million — coming in slightly over due to tariffs and overtime costs. The center includes a large weight room, multiple meeting rooms, goalie training areas, outdoor turf space and three adjacent practice fields, and is described by players as setting a new standard for facilities in the National Women's Soccer League.

The team, which was awarded as the NWSL's 16th expansion franchise about 18 months ago, will begin playing at the adjacent 12,000-seat modular Centennial Stadium next month. That venue will serve as a temporary home for a couple of years until the completion of a permanent 14,500-seat stadium at Santa Fe Yards in Denver.

Controlling owner Rob Cohen said the facility was deliberately designed with expansion in mind, including the possibility of eventually housing a second team. Veteran players including goalkeeper Abby Smith and defender Janine Sonis contrasted the new digs with their early professional years, when teams practiced on rented youth fields or operated out of trailers.

stories.headline: United Airlines to Launch Nonstop Denver-to-Turks-and-Caicos Flights This Winter
stories.summary:
United Airlines announced Friday it will begin nonstop service between Denver International Airport and Providenciales International Airport in Turks and Caicos beginning Dec. 19, operating once weekly on Saturdays through April 24, 2027. The roughly five-hour route on a Boeing 737 MAX 9 makes United the only major carrier offering a direct connection between Denver and the Caribbean archipelago.

The new route brings DIA's total nonstop international destinations to 35 and gives United more than 200 nonstop destinations from Denver, including 24 international cities. It is the fifth Caribbean destination United serves from DIA, joining Nassau, Montego Bay, Punta Cana and San Juan.

The announcement follows diplomatic exchanges between Colorado and Turks and Caicos officials, including a Denver roadshow by a delegation from the islands and Gov. Jared Polis's proclamation of May 20, 2026, as Turks and Caicos Islands Friendship Day in Colorado. United said it has invested more than $1 billion at Denver since 2021.

stories.headline: Daniels Park Bison Herd to Graze Highlands Ranch Prairie for First Time in 150 Years
stories.summary:
Bison from the historic Daniels Park herd will seasonally graze approximately 150 acres of native prairie at the Highlands Ranch Community Association's Backcountry Wilderness Area, marking the first time the animals have occupied that land in more than 150 years. The project was announced jointly by the Highlands Ranch Community Association, Denver Mountain Parks, the Denver Zoo Conservation Alliance and Sanctuary Golf Course.

The Daniels Park herd traces its lineage to some of the last wild bison in North America and descends from animals involved in Colorado's first wildlife reintroduction at Genesee Park more than a century ago. Organizers called the return "an important step toward restoring ecological processes that once shaped Colorado's Great Plains landscapes."

"These bison carry forward a conservation legacy that began more than a century ago," said Shannon Dennison, director of Denver Mountain Parks. The seasonal grazing arrangement is designed to reintroduce the ecological role bison historically played in shaping native grassland habitat on the Front Range.

stories.headline: RTD Rerouting Numerous Bus Lines This Weekend for Denver Pride Events
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District will implement temporary bus route changes and boarding location adjustments throughout this weekend as Denver Pride events close portions of downtown streets. On Saturday, the Denver Pride 5K race at Cheesman Park will affect Routes 10, 12, 8, 19, 32, 48, ART and the 16th Street FreeRide from 9 a.m. to 2:30 p.m.

On Sunday, both Denver PrideFest and the Vizzy Denver Pride Parade will trigger broader disruptions. PrideFest is relocating from Civic Center Park to part of 16th Street this year, with road closures running from 7 a.m. Saturday through 11 p.m. Sunday. The 16th Street FreeRide will divert to 15th and 17th Streets between Lawrence Street and Broadway.

The Pride Parade begins at 9:30 a.m. at Franklin Street and 17th Avenue. RTD said riders should anticipate delays and detours on Routes 0, 1, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12, 15, 15L, 16, 19, 20, 48 and 52, among others. Eastbound 83L bus riders will board on Broadway at 13th Avenue due to Civic Center Park's closure.

stories.headline: Eagle, Pitkin and Lake Counties Hit Worst Drought Level on Record as Fire Danger Surges
stories.summary:
Nearly 97 percent of Colorado is experiencing drought conditions, with Eagle, Pitkin and Lake counties all reaching a Drought Severity Coverage Index score of 500 — the maximum possible — for the first time since records began in January 2000. Eagle County has been at that peak level since late March, according to the U.S. Drought Monitor data published June 25.

The affected counties sit at the heart of Colorado's outdoor recreation corridor, encompassing Vail, Aspen, Breckenridge, Leadville, Glenwood Springs and other mountain communities expecting heavy summer visitation. Surrounding counties including Grand, Summit, Garfield and Routt also have large areas at the most severe drought stage.

Wildfire risk is elevated significantly across the high country heading into summer. Officials are urging visitors and recreationists to follow all fire bans, avoid parking on dry grass, and report suspicious activity quickly. The only areas of Colorado not currently in drought are two small pockets on the Eastern Plains, and forecasters expect conditions to worsen with hot, dry weeks ahead.

stories.headline: Colorado Approves Emergency Fish Salvages at Three Draining Reservoirs
stories.summary:
Colorado Parks and Wildlife has authorized emergency fish salvages at three state reservoirs facing imminent drainage, lifting standard bag and possession limits and allowing all legal fishing methods at each site. The action is intended to reduce fish loss by maximizing angler harvest before water levels drop too low to support the fisheries.

The three affected sites are Wahatoya Reservoir in Huerfano County, being drawn down by the City of Walsenburg for municipal water needs; Mountain Home Reservoir in Costilla County near Fort Garland, being drained by the Trinchera Irrigation Company due to drought and low snowpack; and Douglas Reservoir in Larimer County west of Wellington, being reduced primarily for irrigation demand.

While bag limits are suspended, authorities specified that commercial fishing, net casting and seining remain prohibited. Transporting live fish between bodies of water also remains illegal. The emergency actions are effective immediately.

stories.headline: Former CBI Scientist Missy Woods Pleads Guilty to Four Felonies in DNA Testing Scandal
stories.summary:
Missy Woods, a former scientist with the Colorado Bureau of Investigation, pleaded guilty to four felony counts in connection with a DNA testing scandal that called into question the integrity of forensic evidence used in criminal cases across the state. The case is among the most serious breaches of scientific misconduct in Colorado law enforcement history.

The guilty plea follows an investigation into falsified or improperly handled DNA analyses at the CBI lab. The scandal raised concerns about convictions that may have relied on compromised forensic evidence, and prompted broader scrutiny of laboratory protocols and oversight within the agency.

Details of sentencing were not immediately available. The case has drawn attention from defense attorneys statewide who have sought to review cases in which Woods's work may have played a role.

stories.headline: Hailstorm Season Prompts Warnings About Fly-by-Night Roofing Contractors on the Front Range
stories.summary:
As the Front Range enters its annual hailstorm season — with golf ball- to baseball-sized hail already reported near Wiggins and as far east as Fort Morgan and Evergreen — the Colorado Roofing Association is warning homeowners to be wary of high-pressure tactics from unlicensed or transient contractors. In 2017, a single Denver-area storm generated an estimated $2.3 billion in insurance claims, including approximately 100,000 home damage reports.

Thorn ton-based roofing contractor Mark Dulon said calls from affected homeowners are already coming in, and that less reputable operators consistently emerge after major storms to solicit quick-turnaround repair work. Industry officials advise homeowners to document damage with photographs before contacting any contractor, and to resist pressure to sign agreements immediately after a storm.

The Arvada-based Colorado Roofing Association recommends that consumers verify a contractor has a physical office, local references, current licensing and insurance, and a registration with the Colorado Secretary of State. Written proposals should be obtained before any work begins.

stories.headline: Colorado-Tied Study Finds Brief Lifestyle Changes Yield Health Benefits Two Decades Later
stories.summary:
A new study with Colorado ties found that just a few years of intensive lifestyle coaching — healthier eating and exercise — significantly reduced the likelihood of developing multiple chronic conditions nearly 25 years later, even among participants who had regained weight after the program ended. The research followed approximately 3,000 people with prediabetes enrolled in the late 1990s, including about 120 Coloradans.

Participants who received lifestyle coaching were roughly 27 percent less likely to develop two chronic conditions and about one-third less likely to develop three or more, compared to groups that received medication or no intervention, according to Dr. Dana Dabelea, associate dean of research at the Colorado School of Public Health. Researchers used Medicare records to track about 1,200 surviving, identifiable participants.

The findings surprised researchers, who had not expected lasting differences given that the lifestyle group largely regained weight and reduced exercise after the study's active phase ended. Colorado has 75 sites participating in the CDC's condensed Diabetes Prevention Program, most located in the Denver area. The Colorado School of Public Health plans to continue monitoring participants to assess whether the interventions also affect dementia risk, pending federal funding.

stories.headline: Denver Metro Faces Warming, Drier Pattern After Week of Scattered Storms
stories.summary:
Friday marks the last day of measurable precipitation chances for the Denver metro area following a week of scattered storms, with the National Weather Service forecasting a high near 87 degrees and a 30 percent chance of late-afternoon thunderstorms. Eastern Colorado bore the brunt of the storm week, receiving excessive rainfall and baseball-sized hail, while the metro area experienced less severe impacts.

Forecasters are flagging a significant increase in fire danger heading into the coming week, particularly across Colorado's high country, as the state transitions to warmer and drier conditions. The shift comes as nearly the entire state already sits in drought, and fire weather concerns are expected to intensify through the weekend.

Overnight Friday, Denver is expected to see a low around 61 degrees with breezy winds and little to no precipitation. The National Weather Service describes next week's pattern as "little change" — meaning persistently warm and dry conditions across most of the state.

weather.notable: About 12 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: I like to say I was the kid in school who was bullied by his own imagination.

topStory.headline: U.S. Strikes Iranian Military Sites After Drone Attack on Cargo Ship; Iran Retaliates, Hitting Targets Linked to American Forces
topStory.summary:
The United States military conducted strikes against Iranian missile and drone storage facilities and coastal radar positions on Friday after a drone struck the Singapore-flagged cargo vessel M/V Ever Lovely in the Strait of Hormuz the previous day. U.S. Central Command described the action as a "powerful response" to what it called "unwarranted aggression against commercial shipping" that "clearly violated the ceasefire" between the two countries. Three additional drones launched in the same coordinated attack were intercepted by U.S. forces before reaching the ship.

President Donald Trump publicly condemned the drone strike as a "foolish violation" of the existing ceasefire agreement, saying Iran had taken four shots at the vessel and that one had struck the upper deck. Vice President JD Vance warned that "violence will be met with violence," adding that Iran could resolve disputes over the ceasefire's terms through diplomatic channels rather than military action. Iran has not formally claimed responsibility for the attack on the Ever Lovely, though two anonymous U.S. officials told Reuters that Iranian forces fired on the ship.

Following the U.S. strikes, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said its navy struck locations linked to U.S. military forces in the region and warned that any further American attacks would be met with a broader response. Iran's foreign ministry blamed the "treaty-breaking U.S. regime" for the escalation, while a senior Iranian security official characterized Tehran's actions as "ceasefire management" rather than a violation. Bahrain's foreign ministry separately reported that its territory had come under attack from Iranian drones early Saturday, calling the incident a "flagrant violation" of its sovereignty.

The exchange of strikes comes at a critical juncture in U.S.-Iran relations. The two countries signed a 14-point memorandum of understanding on June 17 that called for an end to hostilities and committed Iran to use its "best efforts" to allow safe passage of commercial vessels through the strait for 60 days while negotiations over Iran's nuclear program and sanctions relief continued. Iranian officials had issued a directive before the attack requiring all commercial vessels to coordinate directly with the Iranian navy, and the Ever Lovely had been traveling along a southern route along the Omani coast rather than corridors designated by the IRGC.

The incident has significant implications for global trade and ongoing diplomatic efforts. The strait was effectively closed following U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran beginning in late February, causing a sharp spike in global oil prices and disrupting shipments of oil, gas, fertilizer, and other commodities. The attack on the Ever Lovely immediately halted International Maritime Organization efforts to escort more than 11,000 stranded seafarers out of the Gulf aboard hundreds of vessels. The United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations also reported Saturday that a tanker sustained damage to its bridge after being struck by an unidentified projectile in the strait, further underscoring the fragility of the current ceasefire.
